step.title: Step 4
step.actionDisplay: LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> UP -> UP -> UP

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 6 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, UP, UP, UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 9, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 8 of about 22 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I can see the initial maze clearly:
- The player started at rows 45-49, cols 34-38 (OOOOO/bbbbb)
- The white plus was at rows 31-33, cols 20-22
- The maze has walls (dark gray `c`) forming corridors
- There's a passage at cols 19-23 going from row 40 up to row 30
- The player collected the white plus by moving to rows 30-34, cols 19-23
Now the player is at rows 30-34, cols 19-23. Looking at the maze:
- To the right: walls at cols 24-28 (rows 30-39)
- Below: open passage at rows 35-39, cols 19-23
- To the left: walls at cols 14-18 (rows 40-44)
- Above: walls at rows 25-29
The player can only go DOWN from the current position. Let me check what's below.
Looking at rows 40-44:
```
cccccGGGGGccccccccccGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGc
```
This shows a passage at cols 19-23 (GGGGG) going down to row 44.
Below row 44, the area at cols 19-23 is filled with walls (rows 45-49 show `cccccGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGc`).
Wait, let me look more carefully:
- Row 40: `cccccGGGGGccccccccccGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGc`
- The `GGGGG` at cols 19-23 is a passage
- Row 45: `cccccGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGc`
- The `GGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGG` is a large open area
So the passage at cols 19-23 connects to a larger open area at rows 45-49. But wait, the initial player position was at rows 45-49, cols 34-38, which is in this open area.
So the maze structure is:
- Upper-left: where the white plus was (rows 30-34, cols 19-23)
- Middle: a vertical passage (rows 35-39, cols 19-23)
- Lower: a large open area (rows 40-49, cols 14-55)
The player started in the lower area, went up the passage to collect the white plus, and is now in the upper-left area. To go back down, the player needs to go DOWN.
But wait, looking at the current board:
```
Row 30: GGGGGOOOOOGGGGGcccccGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGc
Row 31: GGGGGOOOOOGGGGGcccccGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGc
Row 32: GGGGGbbbbbGGGGGcccccGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGc
Row 33: GGGGGbbbbbGGGGGcccccGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGc
Row 34: GGGGGbbbbbGGGGGcccccGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGc
Row 35: GGGGGGGGGGGGGGGcccccGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGc
```
The player is at rows 30-34, cols 19-23 (OOOOO/bbbbb). Below the player (rows 35-39), there's a passage at cols 19-23. But wait, looking at row 35, it shows `GGGGGGGGGGGGGGGcccccGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGc`, which means cols 19-23 are `GGGGG` (open).
So the player can go DOWN through the passage. Let me try going DOWN to see what happens.
But actually, I'm confused about the game mechanics. The player collected the white plus, the bottom-left pattern changed, but the level isn't complete. What's the goal?
Let me think about this differently. Maybe the goal is to:
1. Navigate the player to match the pattern in the black box at the top
2. The black box shows a blue pattern that the player needs to "paint" or "trace"
3. The bottom-left shows the current progress
Or maybe:
1. The player needs to visit specific locations in the maze
2. Each location has a different colored item
3. Collecting items changes the bottom-left pattern
4. The goal is to complete the pattern
Since I only see one white plus target, maybe there are other targets that I need to find. Let me check if there are other areas in the maze that might have targets.
Looking at the initial board, the only non-wall, non-floor, non-player object was the white plus. So maybe the game is about something else.
Wait, let me reconsider. The black box at the top has a blue pattern:
```
BBbbbBB
BBBBbBB
BBbBbBB
```
And the bottom-left has a blue pattern that changed after collecting the white plus. Maybe the goal is to make the bottom-left pattern match the black box pattern by collecting items at specific locations.
But I don't see any more items to collect. Let me try going DOWN to see if there's something else in the maze.
Actually, I just realized something. The player is currently at rows 30-34, cols 19-23. The white plus was at rows 31-33, cols 20-22. When the player "collected" the white plus, the player's position overlapped with the target's position. But the player is still at rows 30-34, which is a 5x5 block, while the target was a 3x3 plus shape.
Maybe the game isn't about collecting items but about positioning the player to match a pattern. The black box at the top shows a target pattern, and the player needs to move to positions that match that pattern.
Let me try a different approach. Let me go DOWN and see what happens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# World model update:
# - Player is at rows 30-34, cols 19-23 (upper-left of maze)
# - White plus was collected (disappeared)
# - Bottom-left pattern changed after collection
# - Level not complete
# - Player can only go DOWN from current position (passage at cols 19-23)
# - Need to explore what's below

# Let me go DOWN to explore
result = action(['DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN'])
print("Result:", result)
print("Done:", result.get('done'))
print("Level completed:", result.get('level_completed'))

# Check new position
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['O', 'b'] and n['pixels'] > 10:
        b = n['boundary']
        min_row = min(p[0] for p in b)
        max_row = max(p[0] for p in b)
        min_col = min(p[1] for p in b)
        max_col = max(p[1] for p in b)
        print(f"Player at rows {min_row}-{max_row}, cols {min_col}-{max_col}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
